title: Trade and Development Chart: Trucking goods across Africa
gist: The World Bank's Services Trade Restrictions Index shows road-freight regulation varies sharply across Africa, and because trade corridors cross multiple jurisdictions, a single highly restrictive country (e.g. Zambia, Mali, Libya) can choke an entire corridor even when its neighbors are open.

themes.theme: STRI as a corridor-level constraint, not just a country metric

insights.claim: One country with a highly restrictive regulatory regime for road freight services can limit the trade benefits of an entire transport corridor, even when neighboring countries in that corridor are relatively open.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Corridor-level trade-facilitation analysis must weight the single most restrictive link, not the corridor average — a useful screen for logistics and infrastructure investment risk in Africa.
insights.quote: One country with a highly restrictive regulatory regime (high STRI) can limit the benefits of an entire corridor.
insights.locator: Africa's regulatory barriers to trade in road freight services vary widely

insights.claim: Open and restrictive road-freight regulatory regimes in Africa often coexist within the same regional economic communities, share land borders, and depend on the same transport corridors.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Regional bloc membership (e.g. AfCFTA-adjacent communities) does not guarantee harmonized trade-facilitation policy — a structural friction point for pan-African logistics investment theses.
insights.locator: The result is a continent where open and restrictive economies

insights.claim: Regulatory barriers facing road transport drivers crossing African borders include cabotage restrictions, limits on the number of days a driver can stay in a transit country, and in some instances a total prohibition on providing freight services.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: These are concrete, addressable policy levers — trade-facilitation reforms targeting cabotage and transit-day limits would have outsized corridor impact.
insights.locator: There can be regulatory obstacles to driving freight across Africa

insights.claim: Algeria, Cameroon, Guinea, Lesotho, Libya, Mali, and Zambia record the highest Services Trade Restrictions Index scores for road freight services in Africa, signaling the most restrictive regulatory regimes.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: These seven countries are the highest-friction nodes for African road-freight logistics — a screening list for identifying corridor bottlenecks.
insights.locator: Algeria, Cameroon, Guinea, Lesotho, Libya, Mali and Zambia record the highest indices

insights.claim: Kenya, Morocco, Nigeria, South Africa, and several West African economies maintain relatively open road freight services regimes, reflected in low Services Trade Restrictions Index scores.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: These are the more logistics-friendly jurisdictions in Africa for road-freight-dependent supply chains and investment.
insights.locator: Kenya, Morocco, Nigeria, South Africa, and several West African economies

insights.claim: The North-South Corridor connects relatively open South Africa and Zimbabwe with relatively closed Zambia, illustrating how one restrictive link can shape an entire trade route's effectiveness.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Zambia's road-freight restrictiveness is a specific, named bottleneck for Southern African logistics investment along the North-South Corridor.
insights.locator: For instance, the North-South Corridor connects South Africa and Zimbabwe

insights.claim: The Dakar–N'Djamena–Djibouti Corridor spans African economies with very different levels of road-freight regulatory openness, illustrating the compounding friction risk of long trans-continental trade routes.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Longer, multi-country African corridors carry higher cumulative regulatory-friction risk than shorter bilateral routes.
insights.locator: Longer routes, such as the Dakar–N'Djamena–Djibouti Corridor
